21.summary: After spring showers, the year's first rainbows arc across clearing skies, their colors soft and fleeting against the pale blue.
21.opening: The rain stops mid-afternoon, and suddenly there it is — a faint arc of color suspended between two hills, so delicate it seems painted on mist. The air carries that particular sweetness of wet earth and fresh leaves, while droplets still cling to every surface, each one holding its own small spectrum. This is the season when sky and water conspire to remind us that light itself can be broken open.
21.nature_notes: Swallows have fully returned now, their forked tails cutting swift arcs beneath clouds heavy with the next shower. Cherry blossoms have given way to the unfurling of fresh leaves — a green so bright it seems to glow from within. Frogs have begun their evening chorus in the paddies, and the first firefly larvae stir in streamside grasses, though their flight is still weeks away.

seasonal_dishes.name: Takenoko gohan
seasonal_dishes.description: Rice cooked with fresh bamboo shoots and dashi, celebrating the tender first harvest that appears only now in mid-spring.

seasonal_dishes.name: Soramame no shioyude
seasonal_dishes.description: Fava beans simply boiled in salted water, their jade-green color and sweet, nutty flavor at their peak before the pods begin to toughen.

21.cultural_note: This period falls within the observance of the eighty-eight nights (hachijū-hachiya) counting from Risshun, marking the approach of the first tea harvest. Farmers watch the skies carefully, as late frosts can still damage tender leaves. The rainbow itself, though celebrated for its beauty, was traditionally considered somewhat inauspicious — a bridge between worlds, beautiful precisely because it cannot last.

haiku.japanese: 雨上がり虹の根元に牛の声
haiku.romaji: ameagari niji no nemoto ni ushi no koe
haiku.english: After the rain — at the rainbow's foot, a cow's low call

21.closing: The arc fades as you watch, its colors thinning to a suggestion, then to memory — and already the next clouds gather on the western ridge.

22.summary: Along riverbanks and wetlands, the first pale green shoots of reeds pierce through muddy water, signaling the wetland's awakening.
22.opening: The grain rains have softened the earth, and now from the dark water of marshes and river deltas, slender reed tips emerge like green brushstrokes on brown silk. The air carries the mineral scent of wet soil and the faint sweetness of new growth. In the early morning stillness, you can almost hear the land breathing as these ancient plants begin their annual reach toward the pale spring sky.
22.nature_notes: Young reed shoots, tender and chartreuse, push through the waterlogged margins of ponds and estuaries, their tips catching the low-angled morning light. Herons have returned to their rookeries in the reed beds, their guttural calls echoing across the water at dawn and dusk. The surface of slow rivers carries pollen dust, creating golden films that swirl with each passage of carp stirring in the shallows below.

seasonal_dishes.name: Takenoko gohan
seasonal_dishes.description: Rice cooked with freshly dug bamboo shoots, their sweetness peaking during these grain rains when the soil is perfectly moist.

seasonal_dishes.name: Sawara no saikyo-yaki
seasonal_dishes.description: Spanish mackerel marinated in sweet white miso and grilled until caramelized, celebrating the fish's spring migration through the Seto Inland Sea.

22.cultural_note: This period falls within the traditional rice seedling preparation season, when farmers flood their nursery beds to germinate seeds. The appearance of reed shoots was historically watched as an indicator that waters had warmed sufficiently for rice cultivation to begin. In some regions, young reed leaves were woven into simple protective charms for the paddies, connecting the wild wetland to the cultivated field.

haiku.japanese: 葦の芽や水面に映る朝の雲
haiku.romaji: ashi no me ya / minamo ni utsuru / asa no kumo
haiku.english: reed shoots rising— / morning clouds reflected / on the water's face

22.closing: In the reed marsh, a single egret stands motionless among the new shoots, both waiting for what the warming water will bring.

23.summary: The final frosts have retreated from the paddies, and the first tender rice seedlings emerge into warming spring air.
23.opening: Dawn breaks without the bite of frost for the first time in months. In the flooded paddies, impossibly green shoots pierce the still water like brushstrokes on silk, each one catching the soft morning light. The earth exhales warmth now, and everywhere there is the quiet industry of growth beginning.
23.nature_notes: Tree frogs have returned to the rice paddies in earnest, their evening chorus swelling as the water warms. Swallows swoop low over the flooded fields, drinking on the wing and hunting the first clouds of midges. The mountains have shed their last patches of snow, revealing slopes furred with the pale green of fresh bamboo grass.

seasonal_dishes.name: Takenoko gohan
seasonal_dishes.description: Rice cooked with tender bamboo shoots and dashi, celebrating the last of the spring harvest before the shoots grow woody.

seasonal_dishes.name: Shirauo no odorigui
seasonal_dishes.description: Tiny translucent icefish served alive and wriggling, a fleeting delicacy as their spring run comes to its end.

23.cultural_note: This is the time for nae-doko, the careful preparation of rice seedling beds that will supply the paddies for June planting. Farmers tend these nurseries with the attention of a calligrapher preparing ink, knowing that the health of these first shoots determines the autumn harvest. In some regions, families still offer prayers to Ta-no-kami, the deity of the rice fields, welcoming the spirit back from the mountains.

haiku.japanese: 霜消えて 苗の緑や 水鏡
haiku.romaji: Shimo kiete / nae no midori ya / mizukagami
haiku.english: Frost gone at last— / the seedlings' green reflected / in the flooded field

23.closing: In the evening stillness, a single heron stands motionless at the paddy's edge, waiting for what the warming water will bring.

en.summary: Peonies open their heavy, fragrant blooms as late spring reaches its most opulent moment before giving way to summer.
en.opening: The air has grown thick with warmth and the sweet, almost intoxicating scent of peonies unfolding in temple gardens and private courtyards. Their petals — layered like silk robes, flushed from palest shell-pink to deepest crimson — seem too extravagant for the modest scale of a flower. This is spring's final flourish, a last gesture of abundance before the rains.
en.nature_notes: Peonies heavy with dew bow toward the earth each morning, their blooms so full they barely hold their form. Tree frogs have begun their evening chorus in earnest, and the first fireflies flicker in damp thickets near streams. Young bamboo shoots have pushed through the soil with startling speed, their pale green tips visible along forest edges.

seasonal_dishes.name: Takenoko gohan
seasonal_dishes.description: Rice cooked with tender bamboo shoots and light dashi, capturing the sweetness of this year's first harvest.

seasonal_dishes.name: Katsuo no tataki
seasonal_dishes.description: Lightly seared bonito, its arrival celebrated as the first catch of the season brings vitality and good fortune.

en.cultural_note: The peony, known as the 'king of flowers,' has long symbolized nobility, prosperity, and feminine beauty in Japanese art. This period also marks the heart of Golden Week, when families travel to ancestral homes and temple grounds are crowded with visitors admiring the last of spring's showiest blooms. Children's Day on May 5th brings carp streamers rippling against bright skies.

haiku.japanese: 牡丹散りて打ちかさなりぬ二三片
haiku.romaji: botan chirite / uchikasanarinu / ni san hen
haiku.english: Peony petals fall — / two, three, overlapping / on the garden stone

en.closing: A single petal drifts to the moss, and somewhere beyond the garden wall, a child's koinobori catches the wind.

ja.summary: 牡丹が重たげに咲き誇り、晩春がもっとも華やかに極まるひととき。
ja.opening: 庭先や寺院の境内に、牡丹の甘くむせ返るような香りが漂いはじめる。幾重にも重なる花弁は淡い桜貝色から深紅まで、まるで絹の衣を纏うかのよう。これが春の最後の贅、雨の季節を前にした花の宴である。
ja.nature_notes: 朝露を含んだ牡丹は、その重みに耐えかねるように俯く。日暮れには雨蛙の声が水辺に響きわたり、湿った茂みでは今年初めの蛍がちらちらと光りはじめる。竹林の縁では筍が土を破り、若緑の穂先をのぞかせている。

seasonal_dishes.name: 筍ごはん
seasonal_dishes.description: 掘りたての筍を薄味の出汁で炊き込み、春の甘みをそのまま味わう一品。

seasonal_dishes.name: 鰹のたたき
seasonal_dishes.description: 初夏を告げる初鰹を軽く炙り、薬味とともにいただく縁起物。

ja.cultural_note: 牡丹は「花の王」と称され、富貴と高雅の象徴として屏風絵や着物の意匠に描かれてきた。この時期は大型連休の只中にあたり、故郷へ帰省する人々や、牡丹寺と呼ばれる名所を訪れる人々で賑わう。五月五日の端午の節句には、鯉のぼりが薫風にはためく。

haiku.japanese: 牡丹散りて打ちかさなりぬ二三片
haiku.romaji: botan chirite / uchikasanarinu / ni san hen
haiku.english: Peony petals fall — / two, three, overlapping / on the garden stone

ja.closing: 苔の上に花弁がひとひら落ち、塀の向こうで鯉のぼりが風を孕む音がする。

en.summary: The first frog calls break the evening stillness, announcing that summer has truly begun in the paddies and wetlands.
en.opening: After nightfall, a single voice rises from the flooded rice field — tentative, then answered by another, and another, until the darkness pulses with song. The air carries the mineral scent of wet earth and young rice shoots. This is the threshold moment: winter's silence has lifted, and the land remembers its ancient chorus.
en.nature_notes: Japanese tree frogs emerge from their hiding places to begin their mating calls, their high keening threading through the dusk. Rice paddies, newly flooded for planting, become mirrors reflecting the last light of day. Swallows dip low over the water's surface, hunting the insects that now swarm in the warming evenings.

seasonal_dishes.name: Takenoko gohan
seasonal_dishes.description: Rice simmered with tender bamboo shoots captures the last sweetness of spring's most prized vegetable before the season turns.

seasonal_dishes.name: Katsuo no tataki
seasonal_dishes.description: Seared bonito, barely touched by flame, arrives with the first strong catch of the season — its iron-red flesh a taste of the warming Kuroshio current.

en.cultural_note: Early May brings the final days of Golden Week, when families return to ancestral villages and the countryside swells briefly with visitors. In farming communities, this is also the critical window for rice planting — taue — a practice still marked in some regions with ritual offerings to ensure a good harvest. The frog's voice has long been heard as a blessing on the water-filled fields.

haiku.japanese: 田に水を引けば夜ごとの蛙かな
haiku.romaji: ta ni mizu wo / hikeba yogoto no / kawazu kana
haiku.english: water drawn to paddies — / and now each night belongs / to the frogs

en.closing: The chorus swells and fades with the passing clouds, and in the spaces between calls, you hear the summer breathing in.

ja.summary: 夕暮れの田んぼから蛙の声が立ちのぼり、夏の到来を告げる頃。
ja.opening: 日が沈み、水を張ったばかりの田から一声、また一声と蛙の鳴き声が重なってゆく。土と水の匂いが夜気に溶け、苗の緑がかすかに揺れる。長い沈黙の季節が終わり、田の声がよみがえる五日間である。
ja.nature_notes: 雨蛙が繁殖の歌を歌い始め、夕闘から夜半にかけて田園に響きわたる。代掻きの終わった水田は空を映し、燕がその水面すれすれを飛びながら羽虫を追う。木々の緑は日ごとに濃さを増し、藤の花房が風に揺れている。

seasonal_dishes.name: 筍ごはん
seasonal_dishes.description: 掘りたての筍を炊き込んだご飯は、春の名残と初夏の気配を一椀に閉じ込める。

seasonal_dishes.name: 鰹のたたき
seasonal_dishes.description: 黒潮に乗って北上する初鰹を軽く炙り、旬の走りの力強さをそのまま味わう。

ja.cultural_note: ゴールデンウィークの終盤、帰省客で賑わった田舎にも静けさが戻り、田植えの準備が本格化する。かつては田植えに先立ち、豊作を祈る神事が各地で行われた。蛙の鳴き声は水の恵みの証とされ、その合唱は稲作の始まりを祝福するものと考えられてきた。

haiku.japanese: 田に水を引けば夜ごとの蛙かな
haiku.romaji: ta ni mizu wo / hikeba yogoto no / kawazu kana
haiku.english: water drawn to paddies — / and now each night belongs / to the frogs

ja.closing: 声が重なり、途切れ、また重なる——その合間に、夏の息づかいが聞こえてくる。

en.summary: After spring rains soften the soil, earthworms surface in garden beds and field edges, their quiet labor marking summer's true arrival.
en.opening: The soil breathes differently now. After overnight rain, you notice the dark, turned earth in vegetable patches glistening with moisture, and the faint mineral smell of worms at work rises through the morning air. This is summer not as heat but as hidden industry — life moving through the ground beneath your feet.
en.nature_notes: Earthworms emerge in the cool hours before dawn, leaving their castings in small spirals on damp paths. Sparrows and thrushes wait at the edges of freshly turned beds, heads cocked. The green of rice paddies has deepened to a dense emerald, and dragonflies — still young, pale-bodied — begin their first circuits over the water.

seasonal_dishes.name: shin-shōga no amazu-zuke
seasonal_dishes.description: Young ginger pickled in sweet vinegar, its pale pink blush and sharp freshness cleansing the palate as temperatures climb.

seasonal_dishes.name: aji no tataki
seasonal_dishes.description: Horse mackerel lightly pounded with ginger and scallion, celebrating the fish's peak fat content in early summer waters.

seasonal_dishes.name: biwa no compote
seasonal_dishes.description: Loquats poached in light syrup with a whisper of white wine, their honeyed flesh turned translucent and jewel-like.

seasonal_dishes.name: ao-mame gohan
seasonal_dishes.description: Rice cooked with bright green peas, their sweetness captured whole, a bowl that tastes of fields in full growth.

en.cultural_note: Farmers consider earthworm emergence a signal that the soil has warmed enough for planting summer vegetables in earnest. In traditional agriculture, the presence of earthworms indicated healthy, living earth — a gift requiring no fertilizer. Some rural communities still mark this period with offerings at local shrines to the ta-no-kami, the deity of rice paddies, asking for fertile ground.

haiku.japanese: 蚯蚓出て土匂ひ立つ朝の庭
haiku.romaji: mimizu idete / tsuchi nioi tatsu / asa no niwa
haiku.english: Earthworms emerge — / the garden at dawn exhales / the scent of turned soil

en.closing: Somewhere beneath the tomato seedling you transplanted yesterday, a worm is already at work, pulling summer down into the dark.

ja.summary: 春雨に緩んだ土から蚯蚓が地上に姿を現し、その静かな営みが夏の到来を土の中から告げている。
ja.opening: 雨上がりの朝、畑の土が黒く湿り、歩くたびに足元からかすかに土の匂いが立ちのぼる。蚯蚓が動いた跡だ。まだ肌寒さの残る時間帯、地中で冬を越えた命がようやく地表へと這い出てくる。夏とは、暑さよりも先に、こうした見えない労働から始まるのかもしれない。
ja.nature_notes: 明け方の畦道には蚯蚓の糞塊が小さな螺旋を描き、それを目当てに雀や鶫が首を傾げて待ち構える。田んぼの緑は日ごとに濃さを増し、羽化したばかりの蜻蛉が淡い体色のまま水面をゆるやかに旋回し始めた。

seasonal_dishes.name: 新生姜の甘酢漬け
seasonal_dishes.description: 薄紅に染まった新生姜を甘酢に漬け込み、気温が上がり始めるこの頃、箸休めに爽やかな辛味を添える。

seasonal_dishes.name: 鯵のたたき
seasonal_dishes.description: 脂ののり始めた鯵を叩いて薬味とともに食す、初夏の海を丸ごと味わう一皿。

seasonal_dishes.name: 枇杷のコンポート
seasonal_dishes.description: 旬を迎えた枇杷を白ワインの香る軽い蜜で煮含め、琥珀色に透き通った果肉を味わう。

seasonal_dishes.name: 青豆ごはん
seasonal_dishes.description: 莢から出したばかりのグリーンピースを炊き込み、畑の甘さをそのまま閉じ込めた一膳。

ja.cultural_note: 蚯蚓の出現は、地温が十分に上がり夏野菜の本格的な植え付けが可能になった証とされてきた。蚯蚓のいる土は肥えた土――肥料を施さずとも作物が育つ、農家にとってありがたい土だ。この時期に田の神へ豊穣を祈願する集落も残っており、見えない土の中の営みに感謝を捧げる習わしが今も続く。

haiku.japanese: 蚯蚓出て土匂ひ立つ朝の庭
haiku.romaji: mimizu idete / tsuchi nioi tatsu / asa no niwa
haiku.english: Earthworms emerge — / the garden at dawn exhales / the scent of turned soil

ja.closing: 昨日植えたばかりの苗の根元、もう蚯蚓が夏を地中へと引き込み始めている。

en.summary: Bamboo groves rustle with new life as the season's first tender shoots push through the damp forest floor.
en.opening: In the half-light before dawn, bamboo groves hold a particular stillness — then a soft cracking, almost imperceptible, as another shoot breaks through leaf litter. The air carries the green scent of growth itself, vegetal and clean. This is the brief window when mōsōchiku yields its sweetest harvest, before the shoots toughen and the moment passes.
en.nature_notes: Mōsō bamboo shoots emerge with startling speed, sometimes growing thirty centimeters in a single day. Bush warblers have found their full voice now, their song threading through the bamboo's vertical shadows. The soil stays damp from intermittent rains, and where light penetrates the canopy, ferns unfurl their fiddleheads in slow spirals.

seasonal_dishes.name: wakatake-ni
seasonal_dishes.description: Bamboo shoots simmered with wakame seaweed in delicate dashi — the meeting of mountain and sea that defines this fleeting harvest.

seasonal_dishes.name: takenoko no tosani
seasonal_dishes.description: Shoots braised with bonito flakes until the umami penetrates each layer, a preparation named for Tosa's famed skipjack fishing grounds.

seasonal_dishes.name: takenoko tempura
seasonal_dishes.description: Freshly dug shoots, sliced thick and fried until the batter shatters against the sweet, milky flesh inside.

seasonal_dishes.name: sansai soba
seasonal_dishes.description: Chilled buckwheat noodles crowned with mountain vegetables — fiddleheads, udo, and kogomi — tasting of the forest floor after rain.

en.cultural_note: Bamboo shoot digging is a cherished rural activity during these days, with families returning to ancestral groves at first light. The tradition honors the belief that shoots harvested before sunrise, while still cool from the earth, possess superior sweetness. In Kyoto, the Ōharano bamboo forests draw visitors who come not only to harvest but to walk the groves and feel the particular energy of bamboo in its most vital season.
en.closing: Somewhere in the grove, another shoot has already risen overnight — tomorrow's harvest hiding in plain sight beneath scattered leaves.

haiku.japanese: 朝掘りの笋ひとつ土の香
haiku.romaji: asabori no / takenoko hitotsu / tsuchi no ka
haiku.english: morning-dug bamboo — / a single shoot still carrying / the scent of earth

ja.summary: 竹林の薄暗がりから、今年最初の筍がしっとりとした土を押し上げる頃。
ja.opening: 夜明け前の竹藪には、凛とした静けさが満ちている。やがて、落ち葉を割るかすかな音——また一本、地表へと顔を出す気配。空気は青くさく、土くさく、生命そのものの匂いがする。孟宗竹の旬はほんのわずか、この五日ほどが最も甘く柔らかい。
ja.nature_notes: 孟宗竹の筍は一日に三十センチも伸びることがある。鶯の声はもう迷いなく、竹の縦縞の影を縫うように響く。時折降る雨に土は湿り、木漏れ日の差すところでは蕨がゆっくりと渦を解き始めている。

seasonal_dishes.name: 若竹煮
seasonal_dishes.description: 掘りたての筍と若布を薄味の出汁で炊き合わせる——山と海の出会いをそのまま椀に。

seasonal_dishes.name: 筍の土佐煮
seasonal_dishes.description: 鰹節をたっぷりまぶして煮含め、旨味を繊維の奥まで染み込ませる土佐伝来の味。

seasonal_dishes.name: 筍の天ぷら
seasonal_dishes.description: 厚めに切った生筍を揚げれば、衣の軽さと中の乳白色の甘みが際立つ。

seasonal_dishes.name: 山菜蕎麦
seasonal_dishes.description: 蕨、独活、こごみを冷たい蕎麦に添えて——雨上がりの山の匂いをそのまま啜る。

ja.cultural_note: この時季、夜明けとともに竹藪へ向かう「朝掘り」が各地で行われる。日が昇る前に掘った筍はえぐみが少なく、格別に甘いとされてきた。京都・大原野の竹林には、収穫だけでなく、竹が最も生気に満ちるこの季節の空気を浴びに訪れる人も少なくない。
ja.closing: 藪のどこかで、また一本——明日の収穫が、落ち葉の下でひそかに背を伸ばしている。

haiku.japanese: 朝掘りの笋ひとつ土の香
haiku.romaji: asabori no / takenoko hitotsu / tsuchi no ka
haiku.english: morning-dug bamboo — / a single shoot still carrying / the scent of earth

en.summary: Silkworms awaken in their trays and begin devouring fresh mulberry leaves, their rhythmic eating filling traditional farmhouses with a sound like gentle rain.
en.opening: The mulberry groves stand heavy with leaf, each tree a deep green cathedral humming with purpose. In sericulture villages, the soft rustle of thousands of tiny mouths on fresh leaves creates a continuous whisper — the sound of transformation beginning. The air carries the faint vegetal sweetness of chewed mulberry, mixing with the warmth of late May mornings.
en.nature_notes: Silkworms, now in their voracious third and fourth instars, consume their body weight in mulberry leaves each day, growing visibly between feedings. Swallows dip and wheel above flooded paddies where rice seedlings have just been transplanted, their reflections doubling in the still water. The hydrangeas are budding but not yet open, holding their color in secret.

seasonal_dishes.name: Biwa no shiroppu-ni
seasonal_dishes.description: Loquats poached in light syrup, their coral flesh turning translucent, capturing the fleeting sweetness of early summer.

seasonal_dishes.name: Kinusaya to ebi no itame
seasonal_dishes.description: Stir-fried silk pod peas with shrimp, the pods still crisp and almost squeaking, dressed simply with sake and salt.

seasonal_dishes.name: Isaki no shioyaki
seasonal_dishes.description: Salt-grilled grunt fish, its skin blistered and crackling, the flesh at its sweetest before the rainy season arrives.

seasonal_dishes.name: Shin-ninniku no醤油漬け
seasonal_dishes.description: New garlic cloves pickled in soy sauce, mild and juicy, without the bite of aged bulbs.

en.cultural_note: This micro-season marks the height of traditional sericulture's most demanding period. Farming families once slept in shifts to feed silkworms around the clock, maintaining the warmth and humidity the creatures required. The phrase 'お蚕様' (okaikosama) — 'honorable silkworm' — reflects the reverence given to these insects that sustained entire regional economies, from the mountains of Gunma to the valleys of Nagano.

haiku.japanese: 蚕の音や夜も絶えざる桑を食む
haiku.romaji: kaiko no oto ya / yoru mo taezaru / kuwa wo hamu
haiku.english: sound of silkworms — / through the night without ceasing / they devour mulberry

en.closing: In the rearing house, a single silkworm lifts its pale head, searching for the next leaf — patient, ancient, already dreaming of silk.

ja.summary: 蚕が目覚め、桑の葉を食む音が養蚕農家に静かな雨のように響き始める頃。
ja.opening: 桑畑は深い緑に満ち、一本一本の木が生命の使命を帯びて佇む。養蚕の里では、無数の小さな口が新芽を食む音が途切れることなく続き、変容の始まりを告げている。噛み砕かれた桑の青い香りが、五月下旬の朝の温もりと溶け合う。
ja.nature_notes: 三齢、四齢を迎えた蚕は、一日に自らの体重ほどの桑葉を平らげ、餌やりのたびに目に見えて大きくなる。田植えを終えた水田の上を燕が低く舞い、水鏡にその姿が映る。紫陽花は蕾を固く結び、まだ色を秘めたまま。

seasonal_dishes.name: 枇杷のシロップ煮
seasonal_dishes.description: 薄い蜜で静かに煮含めた枇杷は、珊瑚色の果肉が透き通り、初夏の儚い甘さを閉じ込める。

seasonal_dishes.name: 絹さやと海老の塩炒め
seasonal_dishes.description: きゅっと音がするほど歯触りの良い絹さやと海老を、酒と塩だけで手早く炒めた一皿。

seasonal_dishes.name: いさきの塩焼き
seasonal_dishes.description: 梅雨入り前のいさきは脂の乗りが絶妙で、皮をぱりりと焼き上げると身の甘みが際立つ。

seasonal_dishes.name: 新にんにくの醤油漬け
seasonal_dishes.description: 掘りたての新にんにくを醤油に漬けると、辛味は穏やかで瑞々しく、箸が止まらない。

ja.cultural_note: この候は、伝統的な養蚕において最も手のかかる時期にあたる。かつての農家は交代で眠り、昼夜を問わず蚕に桑を与え、温度と湿度を保ち続けた。「お蚕様」という敬称には、群馬の山間から信州の谷あいまで、地域経済を支えたこの小さな虫への深い畏敬が込められている。

haiku.japanese: 蚕の音や夜も絶えざる桑を食む
haiku.romaji: kaiko no oto ya / yoru mo taezaru / kuwa wo hamu
haiku.english: sound of silkworms — / through the night without ceasing / they devour mulberry

ja.closing: 蚕室の片隅で、一頭の蚕が白い頭をもたげ、次の葉を探している——静かに、太古から変わらず、すでに絹を夢見ながら。

en.summary: Safflowers unfurl their thistle-like blooms in brilliant orange and yellow, signaling the deepening of early summer warmth.
en.opening: The fields of Yamagata flush with color as safflower heads open under a sun that now climbs high and lingers long. The air carries a green sweetness — rice paddies filled, barley ripening, the earth drinking deeply. Mornings arrive with birdsong layered thick, and by afternoon, the light has a golden weight that presses gently on the skin.
en.nature_notes: Safflowers reach their peak bloom, their tubular florets shifting from yellow to deep vermillion as they mature. Swallows are now feeding second broods, their darting silhouettes constant against pale evening skies. In the paddies, young rice plants have taken firm root, their green intensifying daily as frogs chorus through the humid nights.

seasonal_dishes.name: rakkyo no amazuzuke
seasonal_dishes.description: Crisp pickled Japanese scallions, their sharp sweetness a bright counterpoint to rich summer meals.

seasonal_dishes.name: suzuki no usuzukuri
seasonal_dishes.description: Paper-thin slices of sea bass served chilled, showcasing the fish's clean, delicate flavor at its seasonal peak.

seasonal_dishes.name: ayu no shioyaki
seasonal_dishes.description: Salt-grilled sweetfish, skewered whole, embodying the clear mountain rivers where they now run.

seasonal_dishes.name: shiso to myōga no hiyayakko
seasonal_dishes.description: Chilled tofu crowned with fragrant perilla and ginger buds, a dish that cools the body as humidity rises.

en.cultural_note: Safflower cultivation reaches back over a thousand years in Yamagata Prefecture, where the blooms were harvested to make beni — the precious red pigment for dyeing silk and tinting lips. Picking begins at dawn when dew softens the thorny bracts, and the harvested petals are fermented, pounded, and dried into cakes. This tradition of beni-making is now rare, kept alive by a handful of artisans who still produce the only organic red colorant permitted in traditional cosmetics.

haiku.japanese: 紅花や朝露重き摘み手かな
haiku.romaji: benibana ya / asatsuyu omoki / tsumite kana
haiku.english: safflowers — / the picker's hands heavy / with morning dew

en.closing: In the warm stillness of late May, the safflower's crimson deepens like a slow blush, holding summer's first true heat in its petals.

ja.summary: 紅花が鮮やかな橙と黄に咲き開き、初夏の深まりを告げる五日間。
ja.opening: 山形の野に紅花が色づき始める頃、日はいよいよ高く、長く空に留まる。水を湛えた田んぼからは青い匂いが立ち、大麦は穂を垂れ、大地は潤いを深く吸い込んでいる。朝は幾重にも重なる鳥の声で明け、午後になれば光は黄金の重みを帯びて肌に触れる。
ja.nature_notes: 紅花は盛りを迎え、筒状の花弁が黄から深い紅へと移ろう。燕は二番子を育て、薄暮の空をせわしなく飛び交う。田では早苗がしっかりと根を張り、日ごとに緑を濃くしていく。湿りを帯びた夜には蛙の合唱が絶えない。

seasonal_dishes.name: らっきょうの甘酢漬け
seasonal_dishes.description: 歯切れのよい辛みと甘酸っぱさが、夏の濃い味に清涼を添える。

seasonal_dishes.name: 鱸の薄造り
seasonal_dishes.description: 旬を迎えた鱸を薄く引き、淡白ながら滋味深い味わいを冷たいまま楽しむ。

seasonal_dishes.name: 鮎の塩焼き
seasonal_dishes.description: 清流を遡上する若鮎を丸ごと串に打ち、塩のみで焼き上げる初夏の風物詩。

seasonal_dishes.name: 紫蘇と茗荷の冷奴
seasonal_dishes.description: 香り高い紫蘇と茗荷を冷たい豆腐にのせ、湿気を増す体を内から涼ませる一品。

ja.cultural_note: 山形では千年以上にわたり紅花が栽培されてきた。花弁から取れる「紅」は絹を染め、口紅の原料となる貴重な色素である。摘み取りは棘が柔らかくなる朝露の時刻に行われ、集めた花弁は発酵・搗き・乾燥を経て紅餅となる。今ではごく少数の職人のみがこの技を継ぎ、伝統的な化粧に使える唯一の天然赤色素を守り続けている。

haiku.japanese: 紅花や朝露重き摘み手かな
haiku.romaji: benibana ya / asatsuyu omoki / tsumite kana
haiku.english: safflowers — / the picker's hands heavy / with morning dew

ja.closing: 五月の終わり、紅花の色はゆっくりと深みを増し、夏の最初のまことの熱を花弁のうちに宿している。
